correctAnswer: Counterclockwise torques are positive; clockwise are negative (by convention)
acceptableAlternatives: CCW = positive, CW = negative torque convention
quizQuestion: In AP Physics 1 problems (2D), what sign convention is used for torque?
explanation: Counterclockwise (CCW) torques are positive, clockwise (CW) torques are negative (by convention — it can be reversed if stated). For rotational equilibrium: Στ = 0 means Στ_CCW = Στ_CW. This mirrors the sign convention for angular displacement and velocity: CCW is the positive rotational direction (consistent with the right-hand rule pointing out of the page).
visualDescription: A pivot point with CCW torque arrow labeled positive and CW torque arrow labeled negative
distractors: Clockwise is always positive
Sign depends on the direction of gravity
Sign depends on whether the object rotates
Upward forces create positive torque, downward create negative
Torque has no sign — it is always positive
The sign depends on the moment arm direction
Left-side forces are positive; right-side forces are negative
Torque sign depends on the mass of the object

correctAnswer: m₁ × d₁ = m₂ × d₂ (balanced torques about the pivot)
acceptableAlternatives: force times distance from pivot must be equal on both sides
quizQuestion: What equation must hold for a seesaw to be balanced, where m₁ and m₂ are masses at distances d₁ and d₂ from the pivot?
explanation: m₁g × d₁ = m₂g × d₂ (g cancels) → m₁d₁ = m₂d₂. This is rotational equilibrium: torques about the pivot must sum to zero. A heavier child (larger m₂) must sit closer to the pivot (smaller d₂) to balance a lighter child farther out. This is the lever principle — Archimedes said 'Give me a long enough lever and a place to stand, and I will move the world.'
visualDescription: A seesaw balanced with m₁ at distance d₁ on the left and m₂ at distance d₂ on the right, labeled m₁d₁ = m₂d₂
distractors: m₁ = m₂ always
m₁ + d₁ = m₂ + d₂
m₁/d₁ = m₂/d₂
m₁d₁² = m₂d₂²
m₁ × d₂ = m₂ × d₁
d₁/m₁ = d₂/m₂
m₁d₁ + m₂d₂ = 0
m₁/d₂ = m₂/d₁

correctAnswer: Linear ↔ Rotational: m↔I, v↔ω, a↔α, F↔τ, p↔L, x↔θ
acceptableAlternatives: rotational analogs: mass→I, velocity→ω, force→torque, momentum→L
quizQuestion: What are the rotational analogs of mass, velocity, acceleration, force, and momentum?
explanation: Every linear quantity has a rotational analog: mass m ↔ moment of inertia I; velocity v ↔ angular velocity ω; acceleration a ↔ angular acceleration α; force F ↔ torque τ; momentum p ↔ angular momentum L; displacement x ↔ angular displacement θ. The same equation forms hold: F=ma→τ=Iα, p=mv→L=Iω, J=FΔt→ΔL=τΔt.
visualDescription: A two-column table showing linear quantities on the left and rotational analogs on the right
distractors: m↔ω, v↔I, a↔τ, F↔α, p↔θ
m↔τ, v↔L, a↔I, F↔ω, p↔α
m↔I only; all others are different
There are no useful rotational analogs to linear quantities
m↔L, v↔τ, a↔α, F↔ω, p↔I
The analogy breaks down when motion is circular
m↔I and v↔ω only; the other analogs don't hold
Angular momentum L has no linear analog

correctAnswer: Solid disk: I = ½MR²; Thin ring: I = MR²
acceptableAlternatives: ring has larger moment of inertia than disk of same mass and radius
quizQuestion: For equal mass M and radius R, which has a larger moment of inertia: a solid disk or a thin ring?
explanation: Thin ring: I = MR² (all mass at radius R). Solid disk: I = ½MR² (mass distributed from 0 to R). The ring has I = 2× the disk's I because all ring mass is at the maximum radius. A hollow cylinder will roll down an incline slower than a solid cylinder of the same M and R because of its larger I and thus larger rotational KE fraction.
visualDescription: Side-by-side comparison of a solid disk (I = ½MR²) and a hollow ring (I = MR²) with equal M and R
distractors: Solid disk: I = MR²; Thin ring: I = ½MR²
Both have I = MR²
Both have I = ½MR²
Solid disk: I = ⅔MR²; Thin ring: I = MR²
Solid disk: I = ½MR²; Thin ring: I = 2MR²
The moment of inertia is equal for both shapes
Solid disk: I = ¼MR²; Thin ring: I = MR²
Solid sphere: I = ½MR²; ring I = MR² (confused with sphere)

correctAnswer: Maximum torque when force is perpendicular to the moment arm (θ = 90°)
quizQuestion: At what angle between the force and the moment arm is torque maximized?
explanation: τ = rF sin θ is maximized when sin θ = 1, i.e., θ = 90°. This is why door handles are positioned perpendicular to the door — and why pushing a door at the hinge (θ → 0°, sin θ → 0) barely opens it. Also: zero torque when force is along the moment arm (θ = 0° or 180°). A wrench is most effective when pulled perpendicular to its handle.
visualDescription: A door with three force arrows at angles 0°, 45°, and 90°, showing torque magnitudes increasing with angle
distractors: When force is parallel to the moment arm (θ = 0°)
